You
You are a developer migrating from Git to Jujutsu (jj). You have a repository at `/home/user/project` with some tracked files that should actually be ignored. In jj, untracking a file that is already tracked and adding it to `.gitignore` requires specific steps. Your task: 1. Navigate to `/home/user/project`. 2. Untrack the files `app.log` and `config.local.json` from the repository, but keep them on the filesystem. 3. Create a `.gitignore` file in the root of the repository and add `*.log` and `*.local.json` to it. 4. Describe the current working copy revision with the message "chore: ignore log and local config files". 5. Create a new bookmark named `ignore-cleanup` pointing to this revision. 6. Output the commit ID of the `ignore-cleanup` bookmark to `/home/user/project/bookmark_commit.txt`. Ensure that the working copy is correctly ignoring the files and that they are not tracked in the current revision.

I'm exploring the implications for complex projects, and any edge cases that may arise.\n\n\n","state":"done"},{"type":"tool-executeCommand","toolCallId":"8xZvSFExY6cqId2F","state":"output-available","input":{"command":"jj help restore","cwd":"/home/user/project"},"output":{"output":"Restore paths from another revision\r\n\r\nThat means that the paths get the same content in the destination (`--to`) as they had in the source\r\n(`--from`). This is typically used for undoing changes to some paths in the working copy (`jj\r\nrestore <paths>`).\r\n\r\nIf only one of `--from` or `--to` is specified, the other one defaults to the working copy.\r\n\r\nWhen neither `--from` nor `--to` is specified, the command restores into the working copy from its\r\nparent(s). `jj restore` without arguments is similar to `jj abandon`, except that it leaves an empty\r\nrevision with its description and other metadata preserved.\r\n\r\nSee `jj diffedit` if you'd like to restore portions of files rather than entire files.\r\n\r\nUsage: jj restore [OPTIONS] [FILESETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [FILESETS]...\r\n          Restore only these paths (instead of all paths)\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -f, --from <REVSET>\r\n          Revision to restore from (source)\r\n\r\n  -t, --into <REVSET>\r\n          Revision to restore into (destination)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --to]\r\n\r\n  -c, --changes-in <REVSET>\r\n          Undo the changes in a revision as compared to the merge of its parents.\r\n          \r\n          This undoes the changes that can be seen with `jj diff -r REVSET`. If `REVSET` only has a\r\n          single parent, this option is equivalent to `jj restore --into REVSET --from REVSET-`.\r\n          \r\n          The default behavior of `jj restore` is equivalent to `jj restore --changes-in @`.\r\n\r\n  -i, --interactive\r\n          Interactively choose which parts to restore\r\n\r\n      --tool <NAME>\r\n          Specify diff editor to be used (implies --interactive)\r\n\r\n      --restore-descendants\r\n          Preserve the content (not the diff) when rebasing descendants\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys.
